questions.stem: This diagram shows a single large antigen bearing several distinct labeled sites (A, B, C) approached by a lymphocyte. What principle does the figure illustrate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A given lymphocyte receptor recognizes only one specific epitope of the many present on an antigen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Each antigen contains only a single recognizable epitope

choices.label: C
choices.text: One antigen can activate every lymphocyte in the body simultaneously

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lymphocytes bind antigens non-specifically like pattern-recognition receptors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Antibodies bind the constant Fc region of antigens

explanation.summary: A large foreign molecule displays multiple epitopes, but a particular T-cell (or B-cell) receptor is specific for only one epitope it is programmed to bind, illustrating the specificity of adaptive recognition.
explanation.whyCorrect: A large foreign molecule displays multiple epitopes, but a particular T-cell (or B-cell) receptor is specific for only one epitope it is programmed to bind, illustrating the specificity of adaptive recognition.

questions.stem: This diagram shows rod-shaped bacteria anchored to a host membrane by projecting appendages tipped with proteins that engage host receptors. What is the primary role of these structures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exchanging genetic material between bacterial cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Forming a pore to lyse the host cell membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mediating adherence of the pathogen to host cell surface receptors via adhesins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Providing motility to swim through mucosal fluid

choices.label: E
choices.text: Injecting toxins directly into the host cytoplasm

explanation.summary: Pili are appendages bearing adhesins that bind specific host receptors (e.g., mannose, fibronectin), allowing the microbe to attach to host cells. Adherence is a first major step in establishing infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pili are appendages bearing adhesins that bind specific host receptors (e.g., mannose, fibronectin), allowing the microbe to attach to host cells. Adherence is a first major step in establishing infection.

questions.stem: In panel A a microbe survives inside a resting macrophage, while in panel B a T-cell delivers a signal that enables the macrophage to destroy it. Which adaptive contribution converts the macrophage to the killing state shown in B?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Complement deposition forming a membrane attack complex

choices.label: B
choices.text: T-cell secretion of interferon-gamma cytokine activating the macrophage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Natural killer cell recognition of absent class I MHC

choices.label: D
choices.text: B-cell secretion of neutralizing antibody against the microbe

choices.label: E
choices.text: Release of perforin and granzymes inducing apoptosis

explanation.summary: Many microbes persist in non-activated macrophages. Helper T-cells release interferon-gamma that activates the macrophage, boosting reactive oxygen species and lysosomal killing so the intracellular pathogen is destroyed.
explanation.whyCorrect: Many microbes persist in non-activated macrophages. Helper T-cells release interferon-gamma that activates the macrophage, boosting reactive oxygen species and lysosomal killing so the intracellular pathogen is destroyed.

questions.stem: This diagram shows a bacterium entering a host cell, escaping its vacuole, and propelling itself with a trailing tail of polymerized cytoskeletal filaments to invade the adjacent cell. What mechanism drives this cell-to-cell spread?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Passive diffusion through gap junctions

choices.label: B
choices.text: Microtubule motor transport along the spindle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Actin polymerization behind the bacterium forming a propulsive comet tail

choices.label: D
choices.text: Complement-mediated membrane pore formation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Flagellar rotation pushing the bacterium forward

explanation.summary: Listeria monocytogenes escapes its vacuole using listeriolysin O, then recruits host actin via ActA. Directional actin polymerization forms a comet tail that propels the bacterium into neighboring cells, avoiding extracellular immune exposure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Listeria monocytogenes escapes its vacuole using listeriolysin O, then recruits host actin via ActA. Directional actin polymerization forms a comet tail that propels the bacterium into neighboring cells, avoiding extracellular immune exposure.

questions.stem: This flowchart begins with viral infection producing double-stranded RNA and ends in 'no virus production.' Which secreted mediator initiates the two antiviral enzyme pathways shown?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interleukin-2 driving T-cell proliferation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Histamine increasing vascular permeability

choices.label: C
choices.text: Complement C3b promoting opsonization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tumor necrosis factor triggering apoptosis directly

choices.label: E
choices.text: Interferon, which induces oligosynthetase and protein kinase pathways

explanation.summary: Interferon released by infected cells activates 2'-5' oligosynthetase (activating ribonuclease to degrade mRNA) and protein kinase (inhibiting eIF-2). Both require dsRNA to act, halting viral protein synthesis and blocking virus production.
explanation.whyCorrect: Interferon released by infected cells activates 2'-5' oligosynthetase (activating ribonuclease to degrade mRNA) and protein kinase (inhibiting eIF-2). Both require dsRNA to act, halting viral protein synthesis and blocking virus production.

questions.stem: This diagram shows a pathogen population expressing one surface pilin type (blue), the host generating antibody against it (A), then a new pilin variant (green) emerging that antibody no longer recognizes (C). What immune-evasion strategy is depicted?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Secretion of a protease that cleaves bound antibody

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antigenic variation of surface pili through recombination of pilin gene variants

choices.label: C
choices.text: Production of decoy soluble receptors to absorb cytokines

choices.label: D
choices.text: Downregulation of class I MHC to evade T-cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Capsular masking of surface antigens from phagocytes

explanation.summary: Neisseria places a new pilin coding sequence next to the promoter by homologous recombination, expressing a pilin variant not recognized by existing antibodies. This antigenic variation lets the organism escape the adaptive response.
explanation.whyCorrect: Neisseria places a new pilin coding sequence next to the promoter by homologous recombination, expressing a pilin variant not recognized by existing antibodies. This antigenic variation lets the organism escape the adaptive response.

questions.stem: In the schematic of disease progression depicted here, which host cell population is progressively destroyed, driving the transition to the final stage shown at the bottom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Erythrocytes

choices.label: B
choices.text: CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neutrophils

choices.label: D
choices.text: CD4+ T lymphocytes

choices.label: E
choices.text: B lymphocytes

explanation.summary: The flow diagram traces HIV from mucosal entry through lymph-node dissemination and viremia to AIDS. Progressive depletion of CD4+ helper T cells is the central event; once the count falls below 200/microL the patient is defined as having AIDS.
explanation.whyCorrect: The flow diagram traces HIV from mucosal entry through lymph-node dissemination and viremia to AIDS. Progressive depletion of CD4+ helper T cells is the central event; once the count falls below 200/microL the patient is defined as having AIDS.

questions.stem: This illustration depicts the classic symptom complex (pruritus, jaundice, right upper quadrant pain) of an autoimmune cholestatic liver disease that destroys small and medium intrahepatic bile ducts and has a striking 9:1 female predominance. Which condition is being illustrated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute viral hepatitis A

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic hepatitis C

choices.label: C
choices.text: Primary biliary cholangitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Primary sclerosing cholangitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autoimmune hepatitis

explanation.summary: Primary biliary cholangitis presents with fatigue, pruritus, and RUQ pain, with jaundice and splenomegaly in advanced disease; it has a 9:1 female predominance and targets small/medium intrahepatic ducts.
explanation.whyCorrect: Primary biliary cholangitis presents with fatigue, pruritus, and RUQ pain, with jaundice and splenomegaly in advanced disease; it has a 9:1 female predominance and targets small/medium intrahepatic ducts.

questions.stem: This schematic maps cellular adaptations including hyperplasia, hypertrophy, atrophy, dysplasia, and metaplasia. These adaptive changes typically arise as a response to what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An increase in local blood supply

choices.label: B
choices.text: Repeated or chronic cell stress or injury

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal physiologic aging alone

choices.label: D
choices.text: A brief episode of reversible ischemia

choices.label: E
choices.text: A single random genetic mutation

explanation.summary: Metaplasia and the other reversible adaptations shown occur as protective responses to repeated or chronic cellular stress; if the stressor is removed, the cells can revert to their original type.
explanation.whyCorrect: Metaplasia and the other reversible adaptations shown occur as protective responses to repeated or chronic cellular stress; if the stressor is removed, the cells can revert to their original type.

questions.stem: This summary diagram centers on the organelle shown. When this organelle is damaged, which paired consequences drive the resulting cell injury?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased protein synthesis and decreased ROS

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased ATP and increased reactive oxygen species

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased DNA damage and increased glycolysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased ATP and decreased calcium

explanation.summary: Mitochondrial damage lowers ATP (impairing energy-dependent functions) and raises ROS production, which together damage lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids. Failure to reverse mitochondrial dysfunction marks irreversible injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mitochondrial damage lowers ATP (impairing energy-dependent functions) and raises ROS production, which together damage lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids. Failure to reverse mitochondrial dysfunction marks irreversible injury.

questions.stem: This summary schematic integrates the major mechanisms converging on a single end point. What overarching process do the depicted pathways (ATP loss, calcium rise, ROS, membrane and DNA damage) collectively produce?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cellular adaptation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cell injury

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cell proliferation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Normal cell signaling

explanation.summary: The figure summarizes how decreased ATP, increased cytosolic calcium, reactive oxygen species, and membrane/DNA damage act through multiple downstream effects to cause cell injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: The figure summarizes how decreased ATP, increased cytosolic calcium, reactive oxygen species, and membrane/DNA damage act through multiple downstream effects to cause cell injury.

questions.stem: The tumor cell on the left has lost a specific surface molecule, and as a result the T cell on the right is unable to recognize it. The absence of this molecule most directly impairs which immune function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognition and killing of the tumor cell by cytotoxic CD8+ T cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antibody-mediated opsonization of the tumor

choices.label: C
choices.text: Helper CD4+ T cell cytokine release

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neutrophil chemotaxis to the tumor

choices.label: E
choices.text: Activation of the classical complement pathway

explanation.summary: The tumor is MHC class I-deficient. Because cytotoxic T cells identify targets via antigen presented on MHC class I, loss of this molecule allows the tumor to escape CD8+ T cell-mediated killing.
explanation.whyCorrect: The tumor is MHC class I-deficient. Because cytotoxic T cells identify targets via antigen presented on MHC class I, loss of this molecule allows the tumor to escape CD8+ T cell-mediated killing.

questions.stem: This flowchart shows how a cell responds as injury increases from adaptation through to cell death by necrosis or apoptosis. What primarily determines whether the injured cell adapts and recovers versus dies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The cell's location within the body

choices.label: B
choices.text: The severity and persistence of the injurious stimulus

choices.label: C
choices.text: The cell's embryologic germ-layer origin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether the cell contains a nucleus

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether the cell is haploid or diploid

explanation.summary: The diagram shows a continuum: mild, short-lived injury permits adaptation or reversible injury, whereas severe, prolonged injury exceeds the cell's capacity to recover and drives it to death by necrosis or apoptosis. Thus the intensity and duration of the stimulus is the key determinant.
explanation.whyCorrect: The diagram shows a continuum: mild, short-lived injury permits adaptation or reversible injury, whereas severe, prolonged injury exceeds the cell's capacity to recover and drives it to death by necrosis or apoptosis. Thus the intensity and duration of the stimulus is the key determinant.

questions.stem: This diagram outlines the major biochemical mechanisms of cell injury. A fall in ATP (far left) most directly compromises which cellular process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Formation of the mitotic spindle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Packaging of neurotransmitter vesicles

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ribosomal translation of secreted proteins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Melanin synthesis in melanosomes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Activity of the plasma-membrane Na+/K+ ATPase, causing cell swelling

explanation.summary: ATP is required to run the Na+/K+ ATPase; when ATP falls, the pump fails, sodium and water accumulate intracellularly, and the cell swells. The other mechanisms shown (calcium influx, ROS, membrane damage, DNA damage) act through separate routes.
explanation.whyCorrect: ATP is required to run the Na+/K+ ATPase; when ATP falls, the pump fails, sodium and water accumulate intracellularly, and the cell swells. The other mechanisms shown (calcium influx, ROS, membrane damage, DNA damage) act through separate routes.
